title: 10 best herbal teas for period cramps

remedy_properties: Glycyrrhizin: This compound has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help reduce prostaglandins' ability to cause uterine contractions and alleviate menstrual cramp pain.
Flavonoids: The flavonoids present in licorice tea have been shown to relax smooth muscle tissue, including the uterine muscles that contract during menstruation, reducing cramping and discomfort.
Isoliquiritigenin: This compound has been found to inhibit the production of prostaglandins, which are hormones responsible for causing menstrual cramps, thereby providing relief from period pain.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra, also known as licorice, teas helps with period cramps because of its anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties. The roots of this plant contain compounds like glycyrrhizin, which can help relax the uterine muscles and reduce pain. Additionally, licorice tea has been found to have a soothing effect on the body, reducing stress and promoting overall well-being. By consuming licorice tea regularly, women may experience relief from menstrual cramps, making it a natural remedy for managing period discomfort.
remedy_constituents: Glycyrrhizin: This triterpenoid saponin has anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, which may help alleviate menstrual cramps by reducing inflammation and relaxing the uterine muscles.

Flavonoids (specifically, liquiritin and isolariciresinol): These phenolic compounds have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which may help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation associated with menstrual cramps.

Triterpenoid saponins (specifically, glycyrrhizic acid and glycyrrhetic acid): These compounds have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, which may help alleviate menstrual cramps by reducing inflammation and relaxing the uterine muscles.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in glycyrrhizin, a compound that can help to reduce inflammation and ease pain associated with menstrual cramps.
Leaves: Containing flavonoids and other antioxidants, which may help to reduce menstrual discomfort and alleviate symptoms of PMS.
Barks: Used in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, which can help to soothe and relax the uterine muscles during menstrual cramps.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1-2 ounces of dried roots of glycyrrhiza glabra from a reputable source.
Rinse the dried roots under cold running water and pat them dry with a clean towel.
Combine 1 teaspoon of the dried roots with 8 ounces of boiling water in a teapot.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes or until the liquid reaches your desired strength.
Strain the liquid and discard the solids before serving the tea.

remedy_properties: Menthol: Menthol helps to relax smooth muscle spasms, including those in the uterus, reducing menstrual cramp intensity and duration.
Eucalyptol: Eucalyptol has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation and ease pelvic pain associated with period cramps.
Volatile oils (including menthone and limonene): These compounds have a natural analgesic effect, helping to numb the pain receptors in the uterus and reduce menstrual cramp severity.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Mentha x piperita, also known as peppermint, teas helps with period cramps because of its natural analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties. The active compound, menthol, relaxes the uterine muscles and reduces spasms, providing quick relief from cramps. Additionally, peppermint's natural oils have a calming effect on the digestive system, which can help alleviate nausea and bloating often associated with menstrual cramps. Drinking peppermint tea can offer natural relief and comfort during this time, promoting a sense of well-being and relaxation.
remedy_constituents: Menthol: It acts as a local anesthetic, providing pain relief by numbing the uterine muscles and reducing cramp intensity.
Eugenol: This phenolic compound has anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, helping to soothe and relax uterine contractions that cause period cramps.
Rosmarinic acid: As an antioxidant and anti-inflammatory agent, rosmarinic acid helps reduce prostaglandin production, which contributes to pain and inflammation associated with period cramps.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Mentha x piperita leaves are commonly used to make teas for period cramps due to their high concentration of menthol and other compounds that help relax the uterine muscles.
Stems: The stems of Mentha x piperita contain a significant amount of menthol, which is released when the stems are steeped in hot water, creating a soothing and relaxing effect on the body.
Leaves: The leaves of Mentha x piperita are also rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds that help alleviate menstrual cramps and reduce inflammation in the body.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh mentha x piperita leaves and flowers for the tea in the morning.
Dry the freshly harvested leaves and flowers in a warm place for 2 weeks.
Measure out 2 teaspoons of dried mentha x piperita leaves and flowers per 1 cup of boiling water.
Steep the dried herbs in boiling water for 5 to 7 minutes to release their flavor and aroma.
Strain the tea and drink immediately while it is still hot and fragrant.

remedy_properties: Flavonoids: Hawthorn tea's flavonoids, such as quercetin and rutin, have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce prostaglandins, which are hormones that cause menstrual cramps.
Phenolic acids: The phenolic acids in hawthorn tea, including gallic acid and caffeic acid, have analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects, helping to alleviate period pain and discomfort.
Oleanolic acid: Hawthorn tea contains oleanolic acid, a triterpenoid that has been shown to reduce inflammation and relax uterine muscles, which can help ease menstrual cramps and spasms.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Crataegus monogyna, also known as hawthorn, teas helps with period cramps because of its anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties. The flavonoids and oligomeric proanthocyanidins present in hawthorn tea have been shown to relax the uterine muscles, reducing cramping and discomfort. Additionally, hawthorn tea's diuretic properties help to flush out excess fluids, which can contribute to bloating and cramping. The tea's ability to regulate hormonal balance and reduce inflammation also makes it a popular natural remedy for menstrual relief.
remedy_constituents: Triterpenoids: These compounds have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce inflammation and alleviate menstrual cramp pain.
Flavonoids (including Quercetin): As potent antioxidants, flavonoids help to reduce oxidative stress and inflammation, which can contribute to menstrual cramp severity.
Phenolic Acids (including Chlorogenic Acid): These compounds have anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, which can help to alleviate pain and reduce menstrual cramp severity.
remedy_parts: Leaves: They are used due to their anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which help to ease menstrual cramps and discomfort.
Fruits: They are used because they contain compounds like flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have been shown to reduce pain and inflammation associated with menstrual cramps.
Barks: They are used for their analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties, which help to alleviate period cramps and other symptoms associated with menstrual pain.
remedy_recipe: Gather 1 cup of fresh or dried crataegus monogyna leaves and flowers for the herbal tea.
Measure and combine 2 teaspoons of dried leaves and flowers with boiling water in a heat-resistant cup.
Steep the mixture for 5 to 7 minutes to release the medicinal properties of the plant.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the solids from the liquid.
Serve the tea hot or cold and consume within 30 minutes to 1 hour after preparation.

remedy_properties: Anthocyanins: Highbush cranberry tea contains anthocyanins, which are powerful anti-inflammatory compounds that help to reduce pain and inflammation associated with menstrual cramps.
Quercetin: Quercetin is a flavonoid present in highbush cranberry tea that has natural anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, helping to ease period cramp discomfort.
Vasodilatory compounds: The tea contains vasodilatory compounds like rutin and hesperidin, which help to relax blood vessels and improve circulation, reducing menstrual cramp severity.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Vaccinium macrocarpon, also known as highbush cranberry, teas helps with period cramps because of its anti-inflammatory properties. The tea contains compounds like anthocyanins and flavonoids, which have been shown to reduce prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that cause menstrual cramps. Additionally, the tea's high antioxidant content helps to soothe and relax the uterus, reducing pain and discomfort associated with period cramps. remedy_constituents: Anthocyanins: These powerful antioxidants help alleviate period cramps by reducing inflammation and oxidative stress in the body, which can contribute to menstrual pain.

Quercetin: As a flavonoid with anti-inflammatory properties, quercetin helps ease menstrual cramps by blocking the production of prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that stimulate uterine contractions.

Ellagic acid: This polyphenolic compound has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, which may help reduce menstrual pain by inhibiting the production of inflammatory mediators and protecting against oxidative damage.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Used to make teas for period cramps due to their astringent and anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce menstrual cramp severity.
Fruits: Used to make teas for period cramps due to their high levels of antioxidants and flavonoids that help alleviate pain and inflammation.
Seeds: Used to make teas for period cramps due to their high content of essential oils, which have anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties that help reduce menstrual cramp pain.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1-2 cups of dried vaccinium macrocarpon berries in late summer or early fall season.
Steep 1 teaspoon of dried vaccinium macrocarpon berries in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the liquid through a fine mesh sieve into a cup to remove the solids.
Add honey or other sweeteners to taste if desired for a sweeter flavor profile.
Serve the tea immediately and drink 1-3 cups per day as needed for health benefits.

remedy_properties: Antispasmodic properties: Angelica tea has been shown to relax uterine muscles and reduce spasms, providing relief from menstrual cramp discomfort.
Pain-relieving properties: The active compound, ligustilide, in angelica tea has analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects, helping to alleviate pain associated with period cramps.
Antioxidant properties: Angelica tea's high antioxidant content helps reduce inflammation and oxidative stress, which can contribute to menstrual discomfort and cramping.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Angelica archangelica, also known as angelica, teas helps with period cramps because of its anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties. The tea contains compounds like angelica root extract, which relaxes the uterine muscles, reducing cramping and discomfort. Additionally, angelica tea has a natural ability to ease bloating and water retention, common symptoms associated with menstrual cramps. Its soothing effects can also help to promote relaxation and reduce stress, providing relief from period cramps and allowing women to feel more comfortable and at ease.
remedy_constituents: Ferulic acid: As a phenolic compound, ferulic acid has potent anti-inflammatory properties that help alleviate pain and discomfort associated with menstrual cramps.
Ligustilide: A type of sesquiterpene lactone, ligustilide has a sedative effect on the uterine muscles, reducing spasms and cramping during menstrual periods.
Butylphthalide: A sesquiterpene, butylphthalide has anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that help to relax the uterine muscles, providing relief from menstrual cramps.
remedy_parts: Roots: These are the primary part used for teas due to their high concentration of angelic root extract, a compound believed to help alleviate menstrual cramps.
Leaves: The leaves contain angelic acid, which is thought to have anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe period cramps.
Roots: The roots also contain angelan, a compound that has been reported to have a sedative effect, potentially helping to reduce menstrual cramp severity.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried angelica archangelica roots and leaves in late summer or early fall.
Dry the harvested roots and leaves by tying them in small bunches and hanging them upside down.
Steep 2 to 5 grams of dried angelica archangelica roots and leaves in 250 milliliters of boiling water for 5 to 10 minutes.
Strain the tea through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a cup or teapot.
Store the leftover tea in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 24 hours.

remedy_properties: Estrogen-regulating flavonoids: Fennel tea's flavonoids have been shown to regulate estrogen levels, which can help alleviate symptoms of PMS and menstrual cramps.
Spasmolytic compounds: Fennel's essential oils contain compounds like anethole, which has a natural ability to relax uterine muscles and reduce menstrual cramp severity.

Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties: Fennel tea's antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds help reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the body, which can contribute to period cramps and other PMS symptoms.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Foeniculum vulgare, also known as fennel, teas helps with period cramps because it contains compounds like fenchone and anethole that have natural anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties. These properties help to relax the uterine muscles, reducing cramping and discomfort associated with menstrual periods. Additionally, fennel teas may also help to regulate hormonal imbalances and promote digestion, which can contribute to alleviating period cramps and other symptoms. remedy_constituents: Fenchone: Fenchone, a terpene, acts as a spasmolytic agent, relaxing the uterine muscles and reducing menstrual cramp pain.
Anethole: Anethone, a phenolic compound, has anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that cause uterine contractions and pain during menstruation.
Saponins: Saponins, a type of glycoside, have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic effects, which help alleviate menstrual cramps by reducing uterine contractions and inflammation.
remedy_parts: Seeds: Used due to their high content of anethole, a compound with anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that help alleviate menstrual cramps.
Leaves: Used due to their mild anise flavor and antispasmodic properties, which help to relax the uterine muscles and reduce menstrual pain.

remedy_recipe: Harvest foeniculum vulgare seeds or roots when they are mature and dry them thoroughly.
Crush the dried foeniculum vulgare seeds or roots into a fine powder using a mortar.
Combine 1 to 2 teaspoons of the crushed foeniculum vulgare powder with 8 ounces of boiling water in a cup.
Steep the mixture for 5 to 10 minutes or until the desired flavor and aroma are achieved.
Strain the liquid and drink the tea immediately while it is still warm and fragrant.

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Lavender tea contains anti-inflammatory compounds like luteolin, which can help reduce inflammation and alleviate menstrual cramp pain by blocking prostaglandins' ability to constrict blood vessels.

Antispasmodic properties: The essential oil of English lavender has antispasmodic properties that can help relax uterine muscle contractions, providing relief from cramping and spasms associated with menstruation.

Anxiolytic and calming effects: Lavender tea's anxiolytic (anxiety-reducing) and calming properties can help soothe emotional distress and reduce stress levels, which are often exacerbated by period cramps, promoting a sense of calm and relaxation.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lavandula angustifolia, also known as English lavender, teas helps with period cramps because of its calming and relaxing properties. The tea's bioactive compounds, such as linalool and linalyl acetate, have potent anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic effects that help alleviate menstrual cramps. The tea also has a sedative effect, which can promote better sleep and reduce stress levels associated with period cramps. Furthermore, its antispasmodic properties help to ease muscle tension, providing relief from the discomfort and pain often experienced during menstruation.
remedy_constituents: Linalool: Linalool is a terpene that has a sedative effect, which can help reduce anxiety and promote relaxation, providing relief from period cramps.

remedy_parts: Leaves: Lavandula angustifolia leaves are used to make teas due to their calming and anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe menstrual cramps.

Flowers: Lavandula angustifolia flowers are used to make teas because of their high concentration of linalool and linalyl acetate, which have analgesic and antispasmodic effects that help alleviate period cramps.
Seeds: Lavandula angustifolia seeds are not commonly used for teas.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 20-30 fresh lavandula angustifolia flowers in the early morning to preserve their potency and aroma.
Dry the harvested flowers in a warm and well-ventilated area for 1-2 weeks to remove excess moisture.
Chop the dried flowers into smaller pieces using a sharp knife or herb grinder to increase surface area.
Combine 1 tablespoon of dried lavandula angustifolia flowers with 1 cup of boiling water in a heat-resistant cup.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes and strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve to remove solids.

remedy_properties: Salicylic acid: Found in willow bark tea, salicylic acid is a natural pain reliever and anti-inflammatory that helps reduce menstrual cramp severity by blocking prostaglandins, which cause uterine contractions.

Omega-3 fatty acids: Present in evening primrose oil tea, omega-3 fatty acids help reduce inflammation and alleviate cramps by modifying prostaglandin production and improving blood flow to the uterus.
Magnesium: Found in peppermint tea, magnesium helps relax smooth muscle tissue, including that of the uterus, which can help alleviate menstrual cramp discomfort and reduce spasms.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Camellia sinensis, also known as tea, teas helps with period cramps because of its unique combination of compounds. The tea contains high levels of the amino acid L-theanine, which promotes relaxation and reduces stress levels, helping to alleviate cramp-related discomfort. Additionally, Camellia sinensis contains flavonoids and polyphenols, which possess anti-inflammatory properties that can help to ease menstrual cramps. remedy_constituents: Catechins: Catechins, particularly epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), have anti-inflammatory and pain-relieving properties that may help reduce menstrual cramp intensity.
Theaflavins: Theaflavins, formed during tea fermentation, have been shown to have analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects, which may help alleviate period cramp pain.

remedy_parts: Leaves: Known for their astringent and anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce menstrual cramps and heavy bleeding.

Stems: Utilized for their warming properties, which help to soothe menstrual cramps and promote blood flow.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh leaves and buds from mature camellia sinensis plants at dawn when dew is still present.
Dry the harvested material using a low-temperature drying method such as sun drying or machine drying for 2-3 hours.
Measure out 1-2 teaspoons of dried leaves per 8 oz cup of hot water to create a balanced brew.
Steep the dried camellia sinensis leaves in boiling water for 3-5 minutes to release their essential oils and flavors.
Strain the tea leaves from the liquid and serve immediately while still warm to preserve the delicate flavor and aroma.

remedy_properties: Antispasmodic properties: Lemon balm's antispasmodic compounds, such as rosmarinic acid and borneol, help relax uterine muscles, reducing spasms and cramping associated with menstruation.

Anti-inflammatory effects: The tea's anti-inflammatory compounds, including flavonoids and phenolic acids, may help reduce inflammation and discomfort caused by prostaglandins, which can exacerbate period cramps.

GABA-ergic properties: Lemon balm contains GABA (gamma-amino butyric acid), a neurotransmitter that helps regulate muscle relaxation and anxiety, which can contribute to reduced period pain and improved overall well-being.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Melissa officinalis, also known as lemon balm, teas helps with period cramps because of its natural anti-inflammatory and pain-relieving properties. remedy_constituents: Rosmarinic acid: This phenolic compound helps reduce period cramps by inhibiting the production of prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that cause uterine contractions and pain.

remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves are the most commonly used part, as they contain the highest concentration of essential oils, including citral and linalool, which have analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh melissa officinalis leaves and flowers in the morning to ensure optimal flavor and aroma.
Rinse the harvested melissa leaves and flowers with cool water to remove any dirt or debris.
Steep 1-2 teaspoons of dried melissa flowers or 2-3 teaspoons of fresh melissa flowers in boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the melissa flowers and leaves.
Serve the herbal tea warm or at room temperature and drink immediately to enjoy its calming effects.

remedy_properties: Sedative and antispasmodic compounds: Valerian root contains sedative and antispasmodic compounds that can help relieve menstrual cramp pain by relaxing smooth muscle contractions in the uterus.

Inflammation-reducing flavonoids: Valerian tea's flavonoid content may help reduce inflammation in the uterine lining, which can contribute to menstrual cramps, and also have antioxidant effects to protect against oxidative stress.

Isovaleric acid: Isovaleric acid is a valerenic acid derivative that contributes to the analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties of Valerian root, helping to alleviate menstrual cramps and discomfort.

valepotriates: Valepotriates are a group of iridoid glycosides that have a sedative and anti-anxiety effect, which can help to reduce pain and discomfort associated with period cramps by promoting relaxation and reducing stress.

remedy_recipe: Harvest valeriana officinalis roots in late summer or early fall when the plant is in full bloom.
Wash the harvested roots thoroughly with cold water to remove any dirt or debris.
Dry the cleaned roots in a warm dry place for 7 to 10 days or use a dehydrator.
Use a coffee grinder to grind 1 to 2 teaspoons of dried root into a fine powder.
Steep 1 teaspoon of the ground root in boiling water for 5 to 7 minutes to make a tea.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Maypop teas helps with period cramps because its active compounds, such as magnoliane glycosides and flavonoids, have a natural anti-inflammatory effect that reduces prostaglandins' ability to constrict uterine muscles. remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality dried passiflora incarnata flowers and leaves in amounts of one teaspoon per cup.
Heat filtered water to a temperature of 212 degrees fahrenheit for steeping times of five minutes.
Combine the dried herb with the hot water in a tea infuser or heat-resistant cup.
Allow the mixture to steep for ten minutes and then strain the liquid through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth.
Consume one to two cups of the tea twice daily, one hour before meals.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Marshmallow teas helps with period cramps because of its natural anti-inflammatory properties, which help to relax the uterine muscles and reduce spasms. The tea's flavonoids and saponins work together to ease cramp pain, while also soothing the digestive system and reducing bloating. Antispasmodic properties: The saponins present in marshmallow root have antispasmodic effects, relaxing muscles and reducing spasms that can contribute to period cramps, helping to ease discomfort and pain.

Rhizomes: Contain anti-inflammatory compounds that reduce pain and swelling associated with period cramps.
remedy_recipe: Measure 2 tablespoons of dried marshmallow root althaea officinalis into a clean glass jar.
Heat 1 quart of water to boiling point and pour over the measured marshmallow root.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes to allow the herbal properties to infuse.
Strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a teapot or individual cups.
Let the tea cool slightly before consuming, and enjoy as a warm beverage within the first hour of cramping onset.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Raspberry teas helps with period cramps because it contains compounds that have natural anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce uterine contractions and ease menstrual discomfort. Additionally, raspberry teas' magnesium content helps to calm nervous tension and promote relaxation, further alleviating period symptoms. Magnesium: Raspberry tea is a rich source of magnesium, a mineral that helps relax muscles and reduces spasms, alleviating menstrual cramp discomfort.

Rhyzomes: Rhyzomes, which are underground stems that store energy, possess anti-spasmodic properties that can help relax uterine muscles and relieve cramps.
remedy_recipe: Harvest one teaspoon of dried or fresh rubus idaeus leaves and flowers for each cup of tea desired.
Steep the ingredients in boiling water for 5-7 minutes to release the bioactive compounds.
Strain the mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a teapot or mug to remove solids.
Combine 1/2 teaspoon of dried ginger root with the infused herbal liquid and steep for an additional minute.
Drink one cup of the tea twice daily, starting on the first day of menstruation and continuing for 3-4 days.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Dandelion teas helps with period cramps because it contains a wealth of beneficial compounds that work together to alleviate menstrual discomfort. The tea's diuretic properties help to reduce bloating and water retention, which can contribute to cramp severity. Additionally, dandelion's anti-inflammatory properties may help to relax uterine muscles, reducing the frequency and intensity of contractions. Antispasmodic properties: The antispasmodic compounds in dandelion tea, such as sesquiterpenes, help to relax the uterine muscle and relieve spasms that can contribute to period cramps, providing relief from painful contractions.

Magnesium content: Dandelion tea is a good source of magnesium, an essential mineral that helps to regulate muscle contractions and can help to ease menstrual cramp pain by relaxing the uterine muscles and improving blood flow.

Flowers: Dandelion flowers are a popular choice for making medicinal teas because they have been shown to have a calming effect on the uterus and may help reduce menstrual cramps.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh dandelion leaves or use dried leaf extract for the best results.
Boil one tablespoon of dried dandelion root in three cups of water for ten minutes.
Steep four to six grams of fresh dandelion flowers in boiling water for five to seven minutes.
Combine equal parts dried peppermint leaves and dandelion leaves and steep for eight to ten minutes.
Strain the tea and drink hot or cool it down depending on personal preference within an hour.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Stinging nettle teas helps with period cramps because it contains compounds that help to reduce inflammation and relax muscle spasms, providing natural relief from menstrual discomfort. The tea's anti-inflammatory properties work by blocking the production of prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that cause uterine contractions and lead to cramping. Acidic compounds: The acidic compounds present in stinging nettle tea, such as tartaric acid, malic acid, and citric acid, help to stimulate the body's natural response to inflammation, reducing prostaglandins (hormone-like substances that cause uterine contractions) and subsequently easing cramping.

Omega-3 fatty acids: Stinging nettle tea is rich in omega-3 fatty acids, particularly GLA (gamma-linolenic acid), which are converted into prostaglandins that help to reduce inflammation and relax smooth muscle, providing relief from period cramps.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in calcium, magnesium, and iron, which help to relax uterine muscles and reduce menstrual cramp severity.
Barks: Contain compounds like salicylates and flavonoids that have anti-inflammatory properties, helping to ease cramping and discomfort.
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried urtica dioica leaves for every 8 ounces of boiling water.
Allow the measured urtica dioica leaves to steep in boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup or teapot to remove solids.
Add honey or lemon to taste and stir well to combine with the tea.
Drink the tea immediately after preparation or within 30 minutes of straining.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Pot marigold teas helps with period cramps because its active ingredient, calendula, has anti-inflammatory properties that help to ease cramp-related discomfort. Calendula is believed to stimulate prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that help to relax uterine muscles and reduce spasms, providing relief from menstrual cramps. Additionally, the flavonoids present in pot marigold tea have antioxidant properties that help to reduce inflammation and improve overall pelvic health. This natural remedy has been used for centuries to alleviate period-related pain and discomfort, making it a popular choice for many women.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Pot marigold tea contains flavonoids and other anti-inflammatory compounds that help reduce inflammation in the uterus, alleviating menstrual cramp discomfort.
Antispasmodic properties: The tea's antispasmodic properties help relax uterine muscles, reducing spasms and contractions that can cause cramps, pain, and bloating during menstruation.

Antioxidant activity: Pot marigold tea's high antioxidant content helps protect the body from free radicals that can exacerbate menstrual cramp symptoms, promoting overall hormonal balance and well-being.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of pot marigold have been traditionally used to treat menstrual discomfort due to their anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties.
Seeds: Pot marigold seeds contain fatty acids and flavonoids, which can help reduce inflammation and relax uterine muscles, alleviating period cramps.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 20-25 dried calendula flowers for every 8 oz of water you plan to use.
Combine the dried calendula flowers with boiling water in a heat-resistant glass measuring cup.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes, allowing the flavors and properties to infuse into the water.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the solids.
Allow the tea to cool for at least 30 minutes before consumption to allow the active ingredients to stabilize.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Maidenhair tree teas helps with period cramps because it contains a unique blend of flavonoids, antioxidants, and omega-3 fatty acids that work in harmony to alleviate menstrual discomfort. remedy_properties: Antispasmodic properties: Ginkgo biloba's antispasmodic compounds help relax uterine muscles, reducing menstrual cramp severity and frequency.
Anti-inflammatory properties: The plant's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as flavonoids and terpenes, reduce inflammation in the uterus, which can contribute to menstrual pain.
Vasodilatory properties: Ginkgo biloba's ability to dilate blood vessels helps improve blood flow, reducing constriction of uterine arteries that can cause cramps.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in saponins, which help to reduce inflammation and relax uterine muscles, alleviating menstrual cramp discomfort.
Barks: Containing flavonoids and phenolic acids, barks have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help soothe and calm period cramps.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 2 tablespoons of dried ginkgo biloba leaves from a reputable source or online supplier.
Measure and combine the ginkgo biloba leaves with 8 ounces of boiling water in a medium-sized pot.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes to allow the active compounds to infuse into the tea.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth, discarding any solids and retaining the liquid.
Let the tea cool slightly before consuming within 15-30 minutes of steeping for optimal period cramp relief.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: St John's Wort teas helps with period cramps because it contains flavonoids, which have a natural ability to reduce inflammation and ease muscle tension. The tea's active compounds also interact with neurotransmitters in the brain, increasing the production of serotonin and other feel-good hormones that can help alleviate emotional stress and discomfort associated with PMS symptoms. Additionally, St John's Wort has been shown to increase blood flow and relax the uterine muscles, providing natural relief from menstrual cramps and discomfort.
remedy_properties: Hyperforin: This compound has been shown to have analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects, helping to reduce pain and inflammation associated with menstrual cramps.
Flavonoids: The flavonoids present in St John's Wort tea have antioxidant properties, which can help to reduce oxidative stress and inflammation that may contribute to period cramps.
Tannins: Tannins are a type of polyphenol found in the plant, which can help to constrict blood vessels and reduce blood flow to the uterus, providing relief from menstrual cramp pain.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in flavonoids and terpenes, which help to reduce inflammation and ease menstrual discomfort.
Flowers: Containing hyperforin, a natural pain-reliever that helps to alleviate cramp-related symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest hypericum perforatum leaves and flowers from your garden or a trusted source in the morning after dew has dried for maximum potency.
Measure 1 teaspoon of dried hypericum perforatum herbs for every 8 ounces of boiling water needed for steeping the tea blend.
Combine the measured hypericum perforatum herbs with boiling water in a teapot or infuser and let it steep for 10-15 minutes to release optimal medicinal properties.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup, discarding any solids before serving while still warm for best flavor and efficacy.
Consume the tea within 24 hours of preparation, enjoying two cups throughout the day as needed to help alleviate period cramp discomfort.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Motherwort teas helps with period cramps because of its unique ability to relax the uterine muscles, reducing inflammation and spasms that can cause severe discomfort. remedy_properties: Spasmodic relief: Motherwort's sedative properties can help to ease spasms and cramping associated with menstruation by calming the uterine muscles, allowing for a more comfortable menstrual experience.

Antispasmodic effects: Motherwort contains compounds that have antispasmodic properties, which means it can help to reduce muscle spasms and cramps by relaxing the smooth muscle tissue in the uterus, thereby providing relief from period pain.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Motherwort's anti-inflammatory properties can help to reduce inflammation in the pelvic area, which is thought to contribute to period cramps and other menstrual discomforts, thus providing additional relief.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are valued for their ability to soothe and calm uterine spasms, reducing menstrual pain and cramping.
Leaves: The leaves contain bioflavonoids that help to relax the smooth muscles in the uterus, alleviating discomfort and cramping during menstruation.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality leonurus cardiaca leaves and stems from trusted sources or online suppliers in quantities sufficient for your needs.
Dry the harvested leaves and stems using a food dehydrator on low heat for 8-12 hours or air-drying them for several days.
Combine one teaspoon of dried leonurus cardiaca with boiling water to make a single serving, let steep for 5-7 minutes and then strain.
Allow the tea to cool to a comfortable temperature before consumption, which should be within 30-40 minutes of preparation.
Drink two to three cups of leonurus cardiaca tea daily during menstruation to help alleviate period cramps, bloating and other symptoms.

supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to period cramps that are treated with herbal teas are digestive issues, such as bloating and constipation. Teas like peppermint, chamomile, and ginger help soothe the stomach and alleviate discomfort. Additionally, herbal teas may also be used to treat menstrual migraines and other premenstrual symptoms (PMS) like anxiety and mood swings. The anti-inflammatory properties of these teas can provide relief from cramp-like sensations and promote overall well-being.
intro_desc: Herbal teas for period cramps are natural remedies made from plants that help alleviate menstrual cramps, bloating, and other symptoms associated with PMS. They work by reducing inflammation, relaxing muscles, and regulating hormones. Examples of herbal teas that help with period cramps include ginger, chamomile, and peppermint teas. Drinking these teas can improve lives by providing relief from cramps, allowing women to focus on their daily activities, and promoting overall well-being.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best combination of herbal teas that help with period cramps is a blend of ginger and cramp bark tea, along with a soothing chamomile tea. The anti-inflammatory properties of ginger and cramp bark work together to alleviate cramps and discomfort, while the calming effects of chamomile promote relaxation and reduce anxiety. Adding a hint of peppermint tea can also help to ease nausea and digestive issues that often accompany menstrual cramps.
intro_study: For example, according to a study published by Complementary therapies in medicine, primary dysmenorrhoea is a common complaint experienced by many females in their reproductive years. The study, which reviewed 22 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) of medicinal plants for the treatment of primary dysmenorrhoea, found that most of the evaluated medicinal plants showed evidence of efficacy in relieving menstrual pain in at least one RCT. This suggests that herbal teas, which are often made from these medicinal plants, may be a good option for relieving period cramps, although further research is needed to confirm their efficacy and safety.
